fact: April 17 is exactly 107 days into the year - that's like counting to 107 while hopping on one foot!
In the Northern Hemisphere, earthworms are typically very active around this time, wiggling up to the surface after spring rains to make the soil super squishy.
The sun is climbing higher in the sky each day, making shadows shorter and giving you about 13 hours of daylight in most northern places.
Cherry blossom trees in Japan are often blooming or dropping their petals like pink snow around this time, creating carpets of flowers on the ground.
April 17 usually falls in the middle of Aries season, when the ram constellation is prominent in the zodiac.
Maple trees are typically pumping out their helicopter seeds (called samaras) that spin and twirl to the ground like tiny flying machines.
In Antarctica, this date marks the heart of autumn when penguins are often molting - basically losing all their feathers and looking like fluffy, awkward teenagers.
Spring moons around this time often look extra bright and orange when they rise because of all the moisture in the warming air.
Dandelions are typically exploding into yellow everywhere in northern countries, and each flower head contains up to 2,000 tiny individual flowers - that's a lot of wishes to blow!
April 17 sits exactly 65 days after the spring equinox, which means spring is officially hitting its stride in the Northern Hemisphere.
Migrating birds are often flying overhead in huge V-shaped flocks in North America, honking and squawking their way north like feathered traffic jams in the sky.
The air temperature can swing wildly around this date in northern climates - you might need a jacket in the morning and shorts by afternoon because spring weather is basically having mood swings.
